title: Nebraska wildfires leave ranchers scrambling for forage
full_text:
The devastation caused by four major wildfires and several smaller blazes still burning across western and central Nebraska has left ranchers struggling to find the words to describe the impact. Among the tragedies, Rose Mary White, 86, of Arthur, died March 12 as the fires swept rapidly across the grasslands.

Randy Saner, a Nebraska Extension livestock systems educator based in North Platte near the Cottonwood Fire, spent the past weekend in Oshkosh and Dunning, where he witnessed the fires firsthand. 

He said limited snowfall on March 15 helped contain the northernmost fires, but North Platte saw no snow, colder temperatures and persistent winds. Over the weekend, shifting winds pushed the fire front back on itself in some areas, offering some relief.

“The trouble on Sunday was the cold temperatures,” he said. “Pumps were freezing on the firefighting rigs, so they had to bring in hot water to thaw them out.”

Related:Don’t wait for boot: The science behind optimal winter forage harvesting

Tale of 2 regions

On March 15, while cattle producers in the eastern half of Nebraska dealt with blizzard conditions that dumped snow, brought temperatures down into the single digits and wind gusts in excess of 60 mph, the western half of the state was dealing with historic wildfires that continued to spread out of control. 

The massive Morrill Fire to the north in the Sandhills displaced at least 25,000 head of cattle so far, along with loss of stored hay and forage resources. 

“If we get precipitation this spring, like we did in a previous recent fire near North Platte, those forages will grow back,” Saner said. “But ranchers are going to have to feed cows in a confined setting with stored or purchased hay until they can graze those burned pastures this fall. 

“Here, near North Platte, the burned pastures may come back sooner with precipitation, but ranchers are still going to have to find feed for their cows until they can graze their pastures.”

Herd rebuild hindered

Even with record-high cattle prices, the rebuild of the national cow herd, many say, is slower than normal due to drought. Now wildfires in the heart of cow country further hinder a rebuild. 

“Cattle futures rallied today [March 16] and aren’t far off record highs from last fall,” said Bruce Blythe, commodity editor at Farm Futures. “So the Nebraska wildfires would seem to support the bullish-tight supply story.” 

Drought in the Plains remains a major obstacle to any expansion of the U.S. cattle herd. “The Nebraska fires follow February wildfires that burned over 283,000 acres in northern Oklahoma and southern Kansas,” Blythe said. “With so much pastureland burned, combined with market dynamics, including near-record cattle prices and strong beef demand, it’s difficult to see any meaningful expansion in the overall herd any time soon.” 

Related:Revolutionize your rotation: Why farmers are turning to canola and forages

Blythe noted that record-high cattle prices appeared to be driving modest herd expansion before the fires, with USDA projecting a slight 1% increase in beef replacement heifers for 2025. 

“But even that isn’t considered enough to generate herd growth in 2026 and 2027,” he said. “Live cattle prices are down from record levels posted last fall, but they remain historically high.” 

Ranchers in trouble

Starting March 12, when high winds caused the fires to spiral out of control, ranchers prioritized moving cattle to safety, battling the flames, and safeguarding their homes and ranch structures.

Saner shared a report from a rancher who, while moving cattle away from the fires at night, expressed gratitude that the cattle followed the tractor away from the fire front.

Despite the devastation to ranches, the loss of one human life, livestock losses, and the chaos and displacement of people and animals, the region’s residents have come together to fight the fires and gather resources for recovery efforts.

Related:Forage is an attitude, run in a system

“The thing we have to remember is that the land here is resilient,” Sander said. “Forages will come back with some precipitation.” 

4 main fires 

About 701,000 acres have burned so far in Nebraska. The four major fires include:

Morrill Fire. The largest wildfire in state history burned nearly 550,000 acres, mostly in Morrill, Arthur and Garden counties, and near Lake McConaughey.

Cottonwood Fire. To the east of Morrill, this fire burned about 100,000 acres south of Interstate 80 near Brady and Gothenburg.

Road 203 Fire. This blaze burned nearly 40,000 acres in Thomas, Custer and Blaine counties south of Halsey.

Anderson Bridge Fire. About 16,000 acres burned in Cherry County in the northeast portion of the Samuel McKelvie National Forest southwest of Valentine. 

March 23 webinar to offer forage help

Nebraska Extension is hosting a webinar to help cattle producers make forage decisions in the aftermath of the fires. “Planting Annual Forages Under Irrigation for Grazing and Feed” will be at 6:30 p.m. Mountain time, 7:30 Central time, March 23. 

The webinar, with Nebraska Extension specialist Jerry Volesky, will focus on planting options for cool- and warm-season species. Register for the webinar by emailing Aaron Berger at [email protected] or calling 308-235-3122. A recording of the webinar will be available to those who register. 

A complete list of federal, state and local resources is available at the Nebraska Department of Agriculture.

Where to get help or to donate

Donation centers for hay and fencing supplies, as well as monetary donations, have been set up across the region. This list below is compiled with information from Saner and the Nebraska Department of Agriculture: 

Crusty’s Feed Store, 10 Swanson Drive in Arthur. Call Jason Christensen at 308-764-2259.

Kraupies Northport Lot, 9920 US-385, Bridgeport. Call Del Ray Kraupie at 308-279-1692.

Oshkosh Truck Shop, 400 E. Ave. A, Oshkosh. Call Kirk Schoeneman at 308-241-0809.

Gordon Livestock Market, 108 Foch St., Gordon. Call Cathy Tenes at 308-282-1171.

Grant County Fairgrounds, Hyannis. Call Wyatt Thurston at 682-262-0942.

Heartland Lumber, 410 Main St., Oshkosh. Call Jordan Russell at 308-458-7470.

ProMix Sales and Service, Gothenburg. Call 660-342-5457. 

Maline Seed and Fence, Gothenburg. Call 308-529-0781.

Miller Repair, Maxwell. Call Ivan Miller at 308-530-0502. 

Farm Rescue. Call 701-252-2017.

Other locations and resources may be available, so check with these sources for more information: 

Nebraska Department of Agriculture. If you have hay, feed, fencing materials, equipment and household supplies to donate — or need of such items due to the fires — contact NDA at 800-831-0550. Be prepared to share your name, contact information, and what you have to donate or what resources you might need, along with your location. 

Nebraska Cattlemen Disaster Relief Fund. This fund is actively accepting monetary donations to be distributed to producers who are being impacted by wildfires. Visit nebraskacattlemen.org. 

Call these organizations to offer monetary support to victims of the fires: 

Oregon Trail Community Foundation at 308-635-3393 

Nebraska Sandhills Rancher Fire Relief at 308-778-6562 

Gordon Livestock Market at 605-441-8544 

Western Fire Relief Fund United Way at 308-635-2522 

St. John’s Lutheran Church, Curtis, at308-367-4238 

Flatwater Bank, Ricketson, at 308-537-7181
